---
title: "(ST 352) Lab 7 - One-Factor Analysis of Variance"
format:
  revealjs:
    incremental: TRUE
    scrollable: TRUE
    theme: dark
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: slow
    auto-animate: TRUE
    chalkboard: TRUE
---

## Objectives {transition="zoom" transition-speed="slow"}
- Use R to perform an One-Way Analysis of Variance procedure including a Multiple Comparison method

## Remember Memory Game Activity
- Remember to complete the Memory Game and complete thee Memory Game Survey (Due: Thursday May 30th)

## Today's Example
Researchers wanted to compare the â€śreflectivityâ€ť of four different paints used on highways. Twenty-four sections of highways were randomly selected and one of four types of paints (P1, P2, P3, or P4) was randomly assigned to each section of highway so that each type of paint was used on six sections. After six months, the percent of the original reflectivity was determined and recorded for each paint. The data are in the `paint` data set. The variables are:

- `reflectivity` (percent of original reflectivity after 6 months)
- `type` (type of paint)

## Today's Data {transition="zoom" transition-speed="slow"}
```{r}
paint <- read.delim("C:/Users/Ghcto/OneDrive/Desktop/School/Spring 2024/ST 352 (TA)/Lab 7/paint.txt")
paint
```

## Side-by-Side Box-and-Whisker Plot {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
#| output-location: fragment
boxplot(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint)
```

## Side-by-Side Box-and-Whisker Plot {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
#| output-location: fragment
boxplot(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint, horizontal = TRUE)
```

## Side-by-Side Box-and-Whisker Plot {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
#| output-location: fragment
boxplot(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint, horizontal = TRUE,
        col = "plum")
```

## Side-by-Side Box-and-Whisker Plot {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
#| output-location: fragment
boxplot(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint, horizontal = TRUE,
        col = "plum",
        xlab = "% of Original Reflectivity After 6 Months",
        ylab = "Type of Paint",
        main = "Paint Reflectivity Experiment")
```

## Side-by-Side Box-and-Whisker Plot {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
#| output-location: fragment
boxplot(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint, horizontal = TRUE,
        col = "plum",
        xlab = "% of Original Reflectivity After 6 Months",
        ylab = "Type of Paint",
        main = "Paint Reflectivity Experiment")
axis(2, at = c(1:4), labels = c("P1", "P2", "P3", "P4"), las = 2, cex.axis = 1)
```

## Side-by-Side Box-and-Whisker Plot {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
#| output-location: fragment
boxplot(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint, horizontal = TRUE,
        col = "plum",
        xlab = "% of Original Reflectivity After 6 Months",
        ylab = "Type of Paint",
        main = "Paint Reflectivity Experiment",
        yaxt = "n")
axis(2, at = c(1:4), labels = c("P1", "P2", "P3", "P4"), las = 2, cex.axis = 1)
```

## Why do we want this plot?
- Typically used to visualize the distribution (quantiles) of some categorical variable
  - Used to answer your lab questions :)

:::aside
Questions 1 to 4 can be answered now (if not already)
:::

## Summary Statistics {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
# install.packages("mosaic")
require(mosaic)
```

## Summary Statistics {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
favstats(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint)
```

## Summary Statistics {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
# Alternative Code - Repeat for Each Paint Type
summary(paint$reflectivity[paint$type == "P1"])
```

## Summary Statistics {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
# Alternative Code - Repeat for Each Paint Type
summary(paint$reflectivity[paint$type == "P1"])
length(paint$reflectivity[paint$type == "P1"])
```

## Summary Statistics {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
# Alternative Code - Repeat for Each Paint Type
summary(paint$reflectivity[paint$type == "P1"])
length(paint$reflectivity[paint$type == "P1"])
sd(paint$reflectivity[paint$type == "P1"])
```

## Summary Statistics {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
# Alternative Alt. Code
tapply(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, FUN = summary)
```

## Summary Statistics {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
# Alternative Alt. Code
tapply(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, FUN = summary)
tapply(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, FUN = length)
```

## Summary Statistics {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
# Alternative Alt. Code
tapply(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, FUN = summary)
tapply(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, FUN = length)
tapply(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, FUN = sd)
```

## [Summary Statistics (Comparing Mosaic to Alternative Alt. Output)]{.r-fit-text} {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
# Mosaic Package
favstats(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint)

# Alternative Alt. Code
tapply(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, FUN = summary)
tapply(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, FUN = length)
tapply(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, FUN = sd)
```

## Analysis of Variance {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
aov(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint)
```

## Analysis of Variance {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
paintmod <- aov(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint)
```

## Analysis of Variance {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
paintmod <- aov(reflectivity ~ type, data = paint)
summary(paintmod)
```

:::aside
Questions 5 and 6 can be answered now (if not already)
:::

## [Bonferroni Multiple Comparison Method]{.r-fit-text}
$$
\text{Reject } H_{0} \text{ if } \\[0.25cm]
p < \frac{\alpha}{k} \quad\Rightarrow\quad k \times p < \alpha
$$

:::aside
Where $k$ is the number of pairwise comparisons and $p$ is our p-value
:::

## [Bonferroni Multiple Comparison Method]{.r-fit-text}
$$
\text{Reject } H_{0} \text{ if } \\[0.25cm]
p < \frac{0.05}{6} \quad\Rightarrow\quad 6 \times p < 0.05
$$

:::aside
Our specific problem numbers have been input
:::

## [Bonferroni Multiple Comparison Method]{.r-fit-text} {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
pairwise.t.test(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, p.adjust.method = "none")
```

## [Bonferroni Multiple Comparison Method]{.r-fit-text} {auto-animate="true" auto-animate-easing="ease-in-out"}
```{r}
#| echo: TRUE
# Notice our p-values are 6 (the number of pairwise comparisons) times larger now
pairwise.t.test(paint$reflectivity, paint$type, p.adjust.method = "bonf")
```

:::aside
Question 7 (all questions) can now be answered (if not already)
:::
